title: C1 Advanced (CAE) — Use of English
description: Test sprawdzający znajomość angielskiego na poziomie C1

questions.text: Not only ___ late, but he also forgot to bring the documents.
questions.explanation: After negative adverbials like 'not only', subject-auxiliary inversion is required: 'did he arrive'.

questions.text: It was the manager ___ eventually resolved the dispute.
questions.explanation: In 'It was + noun + who/that' cleft structures, 'who' is preferred when referring to a person.

questions.text: Had she known about the risks, she ___ a different approach.
questions.explanation: Inverted third conditional: 'Had + subject + past participle' replaces 'If + past perfect'. Result uses 'would have + past participle'.

questions.text: The proposal met with ___ opposition from local residents.
questions.explanation: 'Fierce opposition' is the standard collocation. 'Fierce' collocates with opposition, competition, debate.

questions.text: The government insisted that every citizen ___ the right to appeal.
questions.explanation: After verbs like 'insist, demand, recommend', the subjunctive (base form) is used: 'that every citizen have'.

questions.text: Seldom ___ such a compelling performance on stage.
questions.explanation: After negative frequency adverbs like 'seldom', subject-auxiliary inversion is required.

questions.text: The results were mixed, ___ which the team had to reconsider their strategy.
questions.explanation: 'In which' introduces a relative clause referring to circumstances: 'results, in which case/light...'.

questions.text: She found the assignment ___ tedious — it took her the whole evening.
questions.explanation: 'Insufferably tedious' is a strong collocation. 'Insufferably' intensifies unpleasant qualities.

questions.text: The architect's vision, ___ as it was, proved impossible to execute within budget.
questions.explanation: 'Inspired' (adjective) describes a vision that has inspired quality: 'as inspired as it was' — concessive construction.

questions.text: Under no circumstances ___ personal data be shared with third parties.
questions.explanation: After 'under no circumstances', subject-auxiliary inversion is required. 'Should' expresses obligation/prohibition here.

questions.text: The senator's remarks were widely ___ as an attempt to mislead voters.
questions.explanation: 'Interpreted as' specifically denotes attributing meaning or intent to someone's words or actions.

questions.text: No sooner ___ the announcement than protests broke out.
questions.explanation: 'No sooner + past perfect' in inverted structure: 'No sooner had X been done than Y happened'.

questions.text: What ___ her most was the complete lack of transparency from management.
questions.explanation: 'Strike' (what struck her most) means to make a strong impression or to occur forcefully to someone.

questions.text: The treaty was signed ___ the backdrop of ongoing military tensions.
questions.explanation: 'Against the backdrop of' is a fixed prepositional phrase meaning 'in the context of'.

questions.text: They attributed the failure ___ poor communication between departments.
questions.explanation: 'Attribute something to something' is the correct prepositional pattern.

questions.text: The data ___ that further research is needed before drawing conclusions.
questions.explanation: 'Indicate' is preferred in academic writing for data pointing toward conclusions without being definitive.

questions.text: The critic ___ the author's latest novel as a masterpiece of modern fiction.
questions.explanation: 'Hail something as' means to praise something enthusiastically and publicly as outstanding.

questions.text: Little ___ that her discovery would change the course of medical history.
questions.explanation: After 'little' used as a negative adverbial, subject-auxiliary inversion is required: 'did she know'.

questions.text: The policy has come ___ considerable scrutiny from independent watchdogs.
questions.explanation: 'Come under scrutiny/criticism/pressure' is a fixed collocation meaning to be subjected to examination.

questions.text: It goes without ___ that confidentiality must be maintained at all times.
questions.explanation: 'It goes without saying' is a fixed idiom meaning something is so obvious it need not be stated.

questions.text: Her ___ handling of the crisis won praise from all sides.
questions.explanation: MASTER (noun) → masterly (adjective): add -ly to form an adjective meaning 'performed with great skill'.

questions.text: The report highlighted the ___ of the current healthcare system.
questions.explanation: SHORT + COME → shortcoming (compound noun, plural 'shortcomings') meaning a fault or deficiency.

questions.text: He remained ___ committed to his principles throughout the scandal.
questions.explanation: WAVER → wavering (adjective) → unwavering (prefix un-) → unwaveringly (adverb with -ly).

questions.text: The politician's ___ remarks caused widespread outrage.
questions.explanation: FLAME → inflammation (noun) → inflammatory (adjective): describes something that provokes strong reactions.

questions.text: Her ___ approach to negotiations earned her a reputation as a tough bargainer.
questions.explanation: COMPROMISE → compromising (adjective) → uncompromising (prefix un-): unwilling to make concessions.

questions.text: The author's prose style is renowned for its ___ simplicity.
questions.explanation: DECEIVE (verb) → deceptive (adjective): drop -ive and add suffix -ive, meaning 'giving a false impression'.

questions.text: There is a growing ___ that traditional values are being eroded.
questions.explanation: PERCEIVE (verb) → perception (noun): drop -ive and add -ption to form an abstract noun.

questions.text: The new regulations have made the entire process ___ bureaucratic.
questions.explanation: BURDEN → burdensome, but the expected derivation here is ONEROUS (synonym) → onerously (adverb): involving heavy obligation.

questions.text: He was praised for his ___ in finding a solution under pressure.
questions.explanation: INVENT → inventive (adjective) → inventiveness (abstract noun with -ness): the quality of being creative.

questions.text: The ___ of the contract meant that both parties were bound by its terms.
questions.explanation: BIND (verb) → binding (adjective/noun): the -ing participle can function as an adjective meaning 'legally obligatory'.
